descriptionSummary: 2026/27 Estimates for Vote Finance and Fiscal Strategy Report 2026. Hon Nicola Willis, Minister of Finance. The Treasury

chunks.text: Yeah. Rob. No, it's not a name. It wasn't the budget. I think they're called robins. What's the name? We'll call lots of things. Morning, Minister. Good morning. Morning, Ian. Good morning. Glad to have you all with us this morning. We'll hand over to you for some opening comments, and then we'll get into it.

Well, good morning, Mr. Chair, and good morning, committee members. Great to be with you this morning. With me at the table today is Ian Rennie, the Secretary of the Treasury, and I believe Chris Bunny, the Deputy Secretary, is just behind me, as well as relevant officials from the Treasury, who are here to help with your questions if need be.

As the Chair has permitted, I'll make some brief introductory remarks. On budget day, people focus on new spending, which is perfectly understandable. It is important to remember, however, that this is only a portion of total government spending, just as an iceberg has a visible portion above water and a large bulk underneath it.

Next year, core Crown expenses in total, that full iceberg, will total around 155 billion, over 70% of which will go to health, education, welfare, and superannuation. Another 6% will go to servicing the government's debt. And that all adds up to a big number.

Core Crown revenue, mostly from taxpayers, will be around 145 billion. So there is a $10 billion difference between those two numbers. That is the deficit, and we will have to borrow to meet it. In comparison, the amounts we focus on in the budget, like net new discretionary spending of $2.1 billion, are relatively small. But that is the point.

The government's approach is to keep new budget spending tight, forcing a hard look at the underlying 150-odd billion of spending, 150-odd billion, to see where this money could be better spent. And better spent for this government almost always means spending it on our priority areas of health, education, law and order, defense, and infrastructure.

Keeping a tight rein on discretionary new spending and reprioritizing across government means we are continuing to invest in frontline public services, providing temporary, timely, and targeted funding to respond to the fuel crisis, and maintaining a pipeline of infrastructure investments. And at the same time, we are repairing the government's books.

That repair is necessary because there has been a long period of operating deficits and growing debt, and that situation will not resolve itself automatically as the economy recovers. Specifically, the government is aiming through its fiscal strategy to reduce core Crown expenses towards 30% of GDP, to return the headline operating balance measure, OBERGAL X, to surplus by the '28, '29 fiscal year, and to put net core Crown debt as a percentage of GDP on a downward trajectory towards 40%.

Members are aware that Treasury's recommended net debt ceiling is 50% of GDP, and that seems to have bipartisan support, or at least it used to. We are aiming for 40% because the ceiling should never be the target. History shows that one economic shock on its own can push up debt by 20% GDP.

That is precisely the point of having moderate amounts of debt, so it can increase significantly in a crisis or a shock, but not cripple the country with unmeetable debt servicing costs. But once that buffer is used, it does have to be restored again. So how are we tracking against our fiscal goals? The Treasury's budget forecasts show core Crown expenses falling to 30.3% of GDP by the end of the forecast period, well on the way to the 30% target.

OBERGAL X is forecast to return to surplus in the '28, '29 target year, and that is a year earlier than previously expected in the half-year update. And net core Crown debt is forecast to peak at 46.1% of GDP in the '27, '28 fiscal year, again, lower than at the half-year update, then decline to 44.4% of GDP by the end of the forecast period.

In other words, the debt curve finally stops going up and starts going down. We are making progress on the debt mountain. This improvement in the books is reflected in the government's borrowing program. New Zealand Debt Management has lowered its forecast issuance of government bonds by $6 billion over the next four years.

This is, members, the first downward revision to the bond program since 2021. There've been a lot of headwinds to the government's fiscal strategy over the last two and a half years from external economic events over which we have no control. Despite this, the fiscal strategy remains on track, and that's because the government has taken care with public money and made what are tough choices.

Since this is election year, the next round of forecasts will be in the PREFU, the Pre-Election Economic and Fiscal Update. I can announce today that the PREFU will be on Tuesday the 29th of September. That is in the week after the House rises, and is five and a half weeks before the election. I'm happy to take your questions.

Thank you, Minister. We've got Barbara Ann first. Good morning, Minister. I do acknowledge this is your third budget, and to Ian Rennie and the team, I totally acknowledge that difficult budget to put together given the uncertainties. Minister, just having a look at between Budget '24 and '26, how many more people are on the job seeker benefit or emergency benefit between those two forecast periods?
So the place to look is if you go to the, um- The budget economic fiscal update, I think, has the table. Certainly, I have the table available here.

So, yeah. Thank you, Mr. Rennie. So, if we look at the numbers, they are on page 116 of the BFOU. And if we look at the actual numbers for the reference year that you gave was 2024, their numbers on JobSeeker support and emergency benefit were 194,000. As we are here today, the actuals in 2025 were 217, and the forecast for '26 is 227,000.

Yeah. So 98,000 extra. So what's the fiscal cost of 98,000 people on the job seeker's benefit across the period to 2028? Well, again, if you look at the table above that, the welfare benefit expenses, in the BFOU, that sets that out. And I think it is clear to everyone that reducing unemployment has not only a fiscal cost but a significant social cost, and it is the objective of this government that we have as many New Zealanders in work as possible. Which is why we are very pleased that in these forecasts, we are forecasting

220,000 jobs being added to the economy over the forecast period. We're forecasting that while unemployment will peak, partly worsened by the fuel crisis, will peak this year. It is forecast to continue to reduce throughout the forecast period. And that is positive for every New Zealander who will have a job, their families who will be supported through that job and independent income, and it is positive for the government books.

So perhaps, maybe then for Treasury, what is the fiscal cost? Well, it's very clearly set out- Does that include-... Ms. Munce. You can see that in the table welfare benefit expenses, which has the costs of the various benefits, including New Zealand Superannuation, JobSeeker, and emergency benefit.

And those costs are set out in the table above. So, the numbers are available for you. Does that include the accommodation supplement allowance, winter energy payments, additional Working for Families? Yes. That table sets out very specifically the amount expected to go into accommodation assistance, the amount expected to go into Working for Families tax credits and other tax credits, noting this government has made some changes to some of those entitlements.

Specifically, we have increased the maximum accommodation allowance that can be paid to families receiving it. Also, of course, we made a significant decision in our first budget to unwind the indexing of benefits to wages, which had been a decision by the previous government. We chose instead to index them to inflation, which has reduced the cost of those benefits.

So as part of your budget, I think it was last year for Investment Boost, you said that it was going to- Sorry, is this a new primary? It's part of the jobs question. Okay. You said that it was going to increase, I think, 240,000 jobs, and this year it's 220,000. Is that correct? In the BFOU last year, we predicted that over that forecast period, which is, of course, a different forecast period from that in this year's BFOU necessarily.

We forecast that over that forecast period, 240,000 jobs would be created. In this BFOU, as we look ahead over a different forecast period, we're looking at 220,000 jobs being created in the New Zealand economy. And I think that that is positive. It shows that there will be new opportunities for New Zealanders, both those currently in work looking to get a new job opportunity, and those who are currently out of the workforce. And you do see that the unemployment number correlates, that it reduces over that forecast period.

So what is the increase in the working-age population over that same period? I don't have those numbers right here, but what we do have is a breakdown of those numbers I'm sure that we can make available to you. You would expect that there would be an increase in the working-age population over that period.

And sorry, I'm still going. That's all right. How many of those jobs are because of the decisions made in Budget 2026? So directly related to budget decisions as opposed to private market- Well, these things are very-... population moves... as the member appreciates, these things are very difficult to disaggregate because the effect of overall employment levels is a combination of what's happening with the underlying population, what's happening with underlying economic growth, and at the margin, there is also the effect of welfare policies.

It is the case that where governments don't provide adequate incentives for people to move from welfare into work, then you could expect structurally higher levels of unemployment. It is also the case that policies such as Investment Boost, which in the Treasury and IRD's estimation will increase overall potential growth, that with higher overall growth comes more job creation. It's also the case that the investment in infrastructure projects and the view of the Infrastructure Commission for every billion dollars of public

investment per year in infrastructure, around 4,500 jobs are created. And it is the case fundamentally that where an economy is growing and businesses have the confidence to invest either in hiring new staff, expanding into a new market internationally, expanding their business here at home, that with that growth comes new job opportunities. Which is why, as a government, we have focused on a going for growth agenda because we know that the best thing that we can do to support more job creation is to overall grow the economy faster.

So then why have employment forecasts been revised down since the last budget by 82,000 fewer people forecast to be employed in '25, '26? There've been a few events in the Middle East which I'd draw the member's attention to. 82,000- In particular, we have seen a global oil shock that, in the view of the International Energy Agency, is the most extreme the world has ever experienced.

That has led to significant effects for the New Zealand economy. First of all, a downgrading in global growth, including from our trading partners. It has also led to a supply shock in prices, which for New Zealanders they're already experiencing at the pump, and is viewed to increase inflationary pressure, therefore meaning a more contractionary monetary policy stance than may otherwise be the case.

New Zealand is not immune to international events, and there have been significant international events since the last budget update. So the budget also forecast shows that real wage being negative. Why is that? Well, because of the international fuel crisis, which is expected in the immediate future to lift inflationary pressure significantly.

What those forecasts also show is that over the forecast period, real wage growth is expected to accelerate and overall be positive. So at 77,000- Sorry, last one... year '27, 72,000 year '28. Treasury says in their BEFU that it's short-term, the impact from that conflict. How can you reconcile that from your last statement?

Sorry, how can I reconcile what? From your last statement. So you've just said in your last statement because of the conflict- Yes... year '27 it's less than 77,000. '28 it's less than 72,000. It's fewer by 72,000. How does that reconcile with- Are you talking about job numbers? Yes. Because there are always, in an economy, lag effects between an economic shock and the impact on different indicators. It is an orthodox economic view that when you have a price shock, and therefore a growth shock, that the last indicator to recover

typically is unemployment. And so Treasury have applied a very orthodox forecasting model in which they expect that as inflation is brought under control and growth recovers, you will then see employment levels recover and unemployment levels recover. Thank you. We've got a new primary from Dan Goodwill.

Thanks, Mr. Chair. Thanks, Minister, and to your team at the Treasury for coming before us today. Welcome. I want to turn the attention to New Zealand's debt position. Mm-hmm. As we know from table one, debt's currently 191 billion. As a proportion of the economy, it- I've got a voice... peaks in '27, '28.

Mm-hmm. How worried are you about New Zealand's debt position, and how worried do you think New Zealanders should be about our debt position? Well, our debt position is materially different from what it has been historically. It tripled in nominal terms following COVID. Yep. Doubled as a proportion of the economy.

And for reference, if you look back, Bill English, Michael Cullen, you can pick a blue or a red finance minister. The orthodox position was to keep debt under 20% of GDP, and it is now far above that. The thing that's very material, I think, for members to understand is the debt servicing cost that comes with that on an annual basis and the opportunity cost that represents. So I'd draw members' attention to page 115 of the BEFU, which outlines core Crown expenses and shows that this year, debt servicing costs are forecast to be $9 billion. Now,

obviously, that far overshadows any discretionary additional spending decisions the government has made in this budget. It's more than four times our overall operating allowance. To put that number into context, it is enough to fund a year's operations of the defense force, the police force, the customs service, the corrections service, the justice system.

So it is a significant amount of money, and every dollar spent on servicing debt is a dollar not available to be invested in our health system, our education system, and public infrastructure. And so it has been the view of successive governments, and certainly the view of this government, that ensuring that we get debt down to manageable levels is important, not just for our resilience in the world, but also for ensuring that we have manageable debt servicing costs.

Good. Next question is what feedback you're getting from ratings agencies, bond holders, bond issuers, or bond purchasers around the current government's approach on fiscal strategy. Well, both Moody's Ratings and Fitch Ratings have looked at New Zealand's debt and deficit positions and have said that it is essential that we keep to the fiscal strategy, and track a credible path back to surplus and debt reduction. They've put us on a negative outlook, which is to say they've retained our current credit rating, which

is a good one because it has material effects for the cost of borrowing internationally. But they've said that it is incredibly important that we show progress on our fiscal strategy of reducing deficits and debt. If we don't do that, what is likely to occur is that those credit rating agencies would downgrade our credit rating.

And of course, many New Zealanders will have experienced what a downgraded credit rating means. What it essentially means is it's harder to borrow and more expensive to borrow. And so for New Zealand, that would be very material. It would mean more of the taxpayer dollars that we collect would have to go into servicing debt rather than investing in the future that we're all trying to create. So maintaining a prudent position in the eyes of the credit rating agencies is important actually for how much it costs to borrow, for the interest rates we pay, not only as the

government, but also that New Zealand businesses and private borrowers pay. So we do take their estimations of our books very seriously. And final question is you've had to make some tough decisions in this budget, but also the previous budgets. Mm-hmm. Can you give us a sense of if you hadn't have Taking those decisions, what would be the potential impact on debt interest and potential outlook from rating agencies?

Yeah. Look, colleagues will be aware that the approach we have taken across three budgets is to supplement our operating allowances with a significant program of reprioritization and saving. And that has been necessary to keep up with the growing costs that are very real in our health system, our education system, the very real needs to invest in infrastructure for the future.

We continue to have an infrastructure deficit in New Zealand, and this government is determined to repair it. And so, in order to support those objectives, we have made over three budgets, $50 billion worth of reprioritizations. And it is simply the case that in the absence of that, you would be adding $50 billion worth of spending to all of the numbers in these books. It would mean that the deficits would be far deeper. It would mean that the debt would be far larger- Mm... and the debt servicing costs would be much higher.

I'd wager also that we would be in a position as an economy where inflation would be higher, and where interest rates would be higher, too. And, I would note that decisions in the past by previous governments not to rein in its spending, materially contributed to very high inflation rates, which stayed out of target for 33 months in a row, contributed to one of the steepest increases in the interest rate path that New Zealanders have experienced, and very much led materially to the cost of living challenges that we are still working to unwind today.

Thank you. Thank you. This seems to be a hot topic. We've got a few supplementaries, first from Chloe and then Barbara Edmonds. So Minister, I have a parliamentary written question from you in which I asked what the revenue impact of the failed ETS auctions would be. Mm. And you said, and I quote, "The sale of units through ETS auctions does not have an impact on revenue. The Crown receives cash proceeds from the sale of ETS units."

Mm-hmm. "When auctions are forecast to clear and subsequently fail to, this results in lower than forecast cash proceeds with a corresponding impact on net core Crown debt." Can I confirm that your understanding as Minister of Finance is therefore that the failure of ETS auctions results in the Crown taking on more debt? So there's two things happening here.

The first is when ETS units sell at auction, yes, that has a cash impact. On the other side, the sale of a unit also creates a liability for the Crown- Understood... which is reflected in the balance sheet. So, there's a very equal and opposite reaction.

While you get a cash positive impact, you get a liability negative impact on the other side. The point that you are making about the cash picture is real, and of course- Yeah... it is across all areas of government forecasts that there can be differences between what is forecast and what eventuates, and the Treasury always updates that in each of its updates.

So to that effect, we have looked across the failure of all of the ETS auctions in the past term of government, and we've compared forecast NZU cash with receipts with actuals. And what we have come to the conclusion of is that there's an approximate $1.4 billion increase in debt that has been taken on as a result of the failure of the ETS auctions.

Does that correspond with your understanding? Well, I think the simple point that I am working to make to you again, is that that has also, on the other side, been balanced out by a commensurate reduction in Crown liability. So you cannot look at one without the other. If those auctions had cleared at that price, the Crown's liability would be higher. So yes, there has been a reduction in cash receipts, but on the other hand, there has been a reduction in Crown liability.

I also would note that it's very unclear to me whether the member is saying that the Crown should intervene in the ETS market to somehow set a higher price for the auction, which is the necessary flow on from what she's saying. Not at all. And it's our view that actually the operation of the ETS- ...

in a predictable manner is quite important. And the Crown has very limited in its ability to affect that price by intervening in the market. Just to be clear, Minister, about said member's position. Mm. It is that we should have a credible ETS market. Something which many market players, experts have provided commentary on over the past two years stating that government policy has resulted in undermining of confidence in the emissions trading scheme, resulting in the failures of those ETS auctions. That is my position. That is the position of said market.

The other point that I would make is that I very much understand the correspondence between liabilities and those cash proceeds. Mm-hmm. But the point that you were making in your previous answers was that increased debt also comes with increased debt servicing. So therefore, are you not concerned with the increase in debt that has resulted as a result of these failure of ETS auctions? My point is that the liability is reduced on the other side.

I think I've made that clearly. Okay, we've got another supplementary from Barbara Edmonds. Yeah, just very simply, how much has net core Crown debts grown on average during the period 2017 and '23, and what are the forecasts between '23 and '28? So I would turn the member to my favorite table in the BEEFA- Same. It's my favorite, too... which I have many copies of printed, which is at the very back, and that is the time series of fiscal and economic indicators, which goes through all of those measures, including core

Crown debt, and shows that core Crown debt, in percentage terms, was in 2020- 4, 41.7% in 2025. The only year for which we have full numbers for this government is 41.9%. It is forecast in '26 to reach 42.4. And look, I'll save the members the delight of me reading out the rest of the row, but it is all set out very clearly there.

Yeah. So my understanding from the table at the back, which is, again, my favorite too, that as-... a percentage of GDP, excluding New Zealand Super, it's risen from 33.9% to 44.8% between those two fiscal periods that I asked about. Are you- Sorry, what is risen by that? From '23 to '28.

So what-... which measure are you talking about? Sorry? Which measure are you talking about? I'm talking about GDP. It's percent of GDP, net core Crown debt. Right. So net core Crown debt- '23 is- Net core Crown debt in 2023 was- 38 38.6 33.9, excluding New Zealand Super.

Yes. Well- So just my question being- Mm... given that it's risen from, my understanding, 33.9% to 44.8% Mm-hmm... just understanding the assumptions underlying that for that change. Yeah. So, in terms of net core Crown debt, the key issue that we are having to deal with is threefold.

One, as I set out in my opening remarks, the gap between current spending and revenue. Now, there have been material impacts on that due to the delayed recovery that the New Zealand economy has experienced. Two, significant debt servicing costs, which the government has had to meet, which go far beyond what the previous government was having to meet due to the larger amounts of debt that our government inherited.

Three, demographic pressures, which have meant that the cost of New Zealand superannuation is rising by hundreds of millions of dollars each year. Table. A table that I think is quite helpful is if members have the fiscal strategy report with them. We went to some effort to set out on page 22 what has gone into the changes in the deficit forecast between Pre-FUBT '23 and PFUT '26.

Colleagues will recall it's been well canvassed that there was a significant downward revision following the overly optimistic forecast provided in Pre-FUBT 2023. But what that table also sets out is that many of the changes that occurred in the deficit position were essentially to do with underlying economic conditions being different than had been forecast.

Even if you exclude policy decisions to reduce taxation on working people, tax revenue reduced significantly over that period. But what you can also see is that deliberate government decisions to reduce budget packages and allowances has had a material improvement to the size of the government deficit.

Which is to say, in the absence of the deliberate decisions by this government, the deficits would've been far larger, the debt position would be far larger. Okay, we've got still on supplementaries, Dr. David Wilson. Yes. Thank you, Minister, for that explanation. You said it a little bit earlier that we can't outgrow this, and we're just talking about net core Crown debt- Mm... currently at around 41.9 and bouncing around the mid-40s right through to 2030.

Mm. So can you put those two things together for us? And also in terms of that outlook, we're looking at nominal GDP already starting to rise and real GDP as well. Mm. So that kind of indicates growth. Can you sort of tell us what you mean when you say we can't outgrow this? Well, first of all, just let me be very clear that we absolutely want growth, and we are forecasting growth in these budget forecasts. Growth will accelerate throughout the forecast period, reaching 2.3% in the next fiscal year and 3.2% beyond that. That's very positive. That growth brings jobs, higher

incomes, a real improvement in people's material circumstances. Alongside it, it does help the government books in that along with greater growth comes greater revenue for the government. Also, reductions in some of the Crown's expenses. So we essentially need to support fewer people on benefits when there's more growth and more employment. At the same time, however, with growth some expenses continue to grow.

So for example, the case of New Zealand superannuation, that is pegged to the after-tax average wage. So with growth, after-tax average wages will grow faster than would otherwise be the case, and therefore New Zealand superannuation broadly reflects growth across the broader economy. So those expenses won't drop even if we're growing faster.

In fact, those expenses will grow faster too. So there are different aspects to it. And when I say that growth alone won't resolve the deficit, what I mean is that we will need to continue to trim back areas of spending in order to bring our books back into balance. That even if you layer over very high levels of growth into these forecasts, you would still have a gap, which is why our government has committed to an ongoing program of reprioritizations, tight allowances, and very careful management of public money.

So you're saying that superannuation, as a percentage of GDP will rise? Superannuation costs as a line item will continue to grow even as the economy grows, and therefore create an opportunity cost that there are less dollars elsewhere to be invested in health, education, other areas. So the answer to that was yes.

As a percentage of GDP, yes, we're expecting superannuation costs to continue to rise. Okay. Thank you. We've got a new primary from Chloe Swarbrick. Are you committed to meeting our entire nationally determined contribution under the Paris Agreement domestically? Well, we remain committed to the Paris Agreement. As the member is well aware, the Zero Carbon Act sets out emission reduction goals which this government is on track to meet.

Not true. In an unfortunate event without- Parliamentary, um, endorsement. The previous Minister for Climate Change, James Shaw, significantly increased New Zealand's commitment under the Paris Agreement to our national development contribution, well beyond what sits in our zero carbon statute. And the cost of that is significant. Um, and now the, the options for the government to meet that obligation are both through domestic ema- emission reduction efforts- Mm-hmm... or there is the potential, which some seem enamored by, of sending billions-

Absolutely not... of dollars offshore Quite the opposite Um, and our preference has been to focus on domestic emission reduction efforts. If I can, Minister, you'll appreciate I only have so many minutes to ask you these questions, so I'd really appreciate direct answers to these questions. My question was, do you intend to meet the entire NDC domestically? Yes or no. We, we remain committed to the Paris Agreement.

Which means, as you alluded to in your own answer just then, that we either have to do that through domestic action or through offshore mitigation. Do you accept, therefore, that every time the government cuts domestic climate action, that the offshore mitigation bill increases? Uh, no, I don't think it's that black and white, and I think that we are one of several countries who are not on track to meet the NDC.

Uh, that doesn't come into effect until 2030. We don't currently have a legal liability to send, uh, billions of dollars offshore, and I suspect that there will be many developments globally a- about how NDCs are met and managed, uh, over the coming years. Are you implying there, Minister, that you expect the Paris Agreement to effectively fall apart? And if so- No... what... Okay, so just, just to be clear then, what developments... Can you give us any specificity?

What are these supposed developments that might occur? If not, the basic mathematics, that if we have a nationally determined contribution, we either have to meet that through domestic action or through offshore mitigation, and those two things correspond. If you decrease one, you increase the other. Well, what I observe is that there are a number of countries internationally- Mm-hmm... who are not on track to meet the NDC requirements- Mm-hmm... while also making good progress in reducing their emissions in their home

jurisdictions. Uh, I think that many of those countries will enter discussions over the next few years about what that means in terms of the Paris Agreement and how we collectively continue efforts globally, uh, to reduce emissions. But in a way that also meets the very real, pressing domestic concerns of a number of nations. Which is to say, I do not think New Zealand is alone in saying that it would not meet the expectations of New Zealand taxpayers if we were to send billions of dollars offshore, uh, to meet the terms of a global agreement. Now, we, New Zealand is not alone in that.

Certainly, there are voters around the world who would feel very uncomfortable about those sorts of choices. And so what I expect is that ongoing diplomatic efforts will continue. To be very clear- Last sup, please. Yeah, I guess just to be very clear, Minister, given that you've implied my position many times in your answers there. The Green Party very strongly- I, I think I'll be read by my words rather than you, h- the implications you take from things. 'Cause in my experience, the implications you take from things are

very different from the implications I take from things. I would like to put my own words in my own mouth, which is- Sure, you do that, and I'll do the same. Uh, absolutely. Great. So I guess the final point that I would make, or rather the question that I was a- I would ask, is do you think that it constitutes good economic management to be making explicit commitments, particularly on the international stage, that you are not budgeting for nor planning to actually meet?

I'm very confident that the Treasury, uh, is meeting all of its obligations in terms of the way that liabilities and commitments are represented in the government books that has been assessed by the Auditor-General, who concurs. Thank you. Uh, we've got another supplementary on this from Dr. Deborah Russell. Thank you, um, Ryan. Um, Minister, thank you for those comments.

Um, just in respect of this then, if we're not going to, um, look at meeting our NDCs through buying offshore mitigation, um, then we would be need, needing to do more onshore. Yep. Can you, can you point to specific initiatives in this budget which will help to reduce our emissions onshore?

Well, uh, the member, uh, is, um... Uh, look, I'll just state it again clearly. This government and this term of Parliament is not going to be sending any money offshore to meet Paris- Sure. So that was my, my question-... obligations. In terms of emission reduction, as I set out in my answer to, um, Chlöe Swarbrick, uh, we are on track through our emission reduction plans to meet our zero carbon targets.

That's not true. And that is through a combination, uh, of activities by the government. It is partly a result of the ETS and the price signals that sends through the economy, uh, and it is partly through the emission reduction efforts that are occurring across the economy. With respect, Minister, the ETS is not sending sufficient signals at the moment, um, given the price it's at. So what I was asking for was a very specific question as to what new initiatives, if any- Mm... that you can point to in this budget that go to reducing-

Yes... our onshore emissions. Absolutely. Well, the number one thing that I think, uh, matters is the way that we are supporting the resource management system to better consent, permit, and fast-track renewable energy projects. Uh, specifically, there is, uh, a line item in this budget to support, uh, a better Resource Management Act system going forward. It has been the case that with the Fasttrack Act, we have been able to bring on stream more solar and wind development than occurred under the last government.

And what we want to see is a Resource Management Act that is able to do that in normal times, not just with, uh, a Fasttrack Act, and we are investing in this budget to support that. We also, in this budget, have put forward a gas transition loan scheme, uh, which is to allow for the fact, uh, that many firms have faced spiking, uh, gas prices as a result, uh, in part of the previous government's decision to ban the exploration of oil and gas.

Uh, and so we are providing Crown-backed, uh, lending to support those firms to transition, uh, in many cases, to electricity, uh, which will largely be from renewable sources. So a- again, with respect, Minister, um, the The previous government had a fast track act that was also getting renewables built, and there were renewables consent- Not as fast as we've done, Deborah.

Well, there's nothing- Well- So that's a moot point. Those things were actually there- Well, it's not moot. There's real solar panels and real windmills as a result of our fast track act. But they also look inward and they also consider, do I think this is going to be a stable environment in which to invest and grow?

And so that growth figure represents the view of what opportunity will be in this economy and what growth it is possible to occur. So how do you reconcile that, given unemployment figures are expected to go higher? Well, as I set out in earlier answers to the members' questions, it is an orthodox economic view that there is an order of events that occurs. Inflation comes down, interest rates come down, growth recovers. Unemployment is the last thing to recover. As I say, a huge driver for me and why I want to see economic growth recovering and accelerating is that

I know that what comes with that is job creation and lower rates of unemployment. And it is the case that that, across any orthodoxy of economic forecasts, will lag economic growth recovery. Deborah Russell. Thank you, Minister. So, you've talked about confidence of businesses to invest given the economic settings and so forth.

Mm-hmm. And you've also talked about the importance of science and innovation- Mm... to this economy. So I just find those pretty high-level claims hard to stack up with what something that's sitting in the supplementary estimates of appropriation, and that's a $25 million reduction in the R&D tax incentive, which is a downwards forecast, saying that we're not going to spend as much on it as we were going to.

Okay, I'll test that explanation with the Minister of Revenue later on. Thank you. Very good. We have a new primary. Chloe Swarbrick. Public sector job cuts. Could you walk us through the logic, the assumptions, and the calculations that were necessary to land you on that 8,700? Okay. Yeah, so the basic logic is that if you look back over time, again, blue and red governments, we're yet to have a green government. God give me strength.

But if you look back- You can look forward to it... over blue and red governments, then proportionate to the population, the core public service has tended to be about 1% of that population. Under the last government, there was an extreme increase in the numbers in the public service, from around 43 to 67,000.

Sorry, no, I've got that wrong. 43 to 61. And what that meant was that as a proportion of the population, core public service was more like 1.2%. As the member is aware, in our budgets, we have consistently looked to reprioritize baselines. We made a very significant baseline reduction in our first budget.

We set caps on contracting and consulting spend. And even despite making those changes, we haven't seen a significant reduction in the number of public servants. In fact, in some areas it has grown. So our view has been that we need to be quite clear that we want it in the logic to look more like what it has historically.

We see no reason why, in a modern age with great digital tools available, why we shouldn't be able to have a core public service similar to size it's been in the past. So we therefore projected forward and said, what would 1% of the population look like? We were realistic. We said, well, you wouldn't achieve that overnight.

The better way to achieve that would be to give yourself some time to plan for it. So we set that out-year target. And then what we've said is, okay, well, how do you achieve that? Because you can achieve that in small beer ways, or you could actually provide to the public service the opportunity to take a blank sheet of paper and say, well, how do we reorganize this thing in a way that leads to less duplication, less wasted resource, is more sensible.

And it's really clear that 39 government agencies, as we currently have, makes us stand out from other countries in the world. It seems like too many. It actually is frustrating both for the agencies and public servants themselves and for policymakers because you have a non-joined-up back office, you have duplicated functions, you have inefficiency.

So in the first instance, we've said to the Secretary for the Treasury, the head of DPMC, and the head of PSC, we want you to provide us, based on the input from all of those government chief executives, a view on how we could reorganize this, make it a better, more efficient system And then from that, we expect that there will be a flow and a reduction in some of the roles that will bring us more back to 1%.

So just to be really clear and to not conflate things there, you're talking about the basic rationale being the extrapolation of the historical size of the public service, not looking at, for example, the application of the efficacy, the capacity of AI tools. Well, let's go back even a further step, which is we're looking at the government books and saying we can't stay in perpetual deficit. We can't keep growing the debt mountain.

But we know we need to keep delivering in every budget more resources to the front line of our public services, more resources for our hospitals, for our primary carers, for our schools, for our early childhood centers. We need to be investing in more infrastructure. How are we going to achieve that if a greater and greater proportion of resource is being sucked out by the back office of the core public service?

We don't think those two things are reconcilable. So we've said a logical position is to take the size of the core public service back to where it's been historically as a proportion of the population. That is the key driver. What you're asking then about is what are the enablers of that shift, and one of the enablers is obviously reducing duplication, ensuring more efficiency, adopting digital tools, which will include AI, in some instances.

Will those cuts under your government continue to move ahead if there is- Mm-hmm... no guarantee that the capacity of the public service is able to remain the same? I take a much more optimistic view than you. The capacity of the public service has not diminished since it last ran with 1% of the population. In fact-... we have incredibly capable people in the public service who believe that they can deliver more with less, and who have said that actually, if you had less duplication and less of this, spaghett that has arisen, frankly under successive governments,

where we've got all of these different agencies and different reporting functions- Mm-hmm... and different back offices. If we had less of that and we're more able to focus on our core job of delivering services to the public in a logical, lean structure, then that would be a good thing for New Zealanders. Respectfully- And I concur with that view. Respectfully, Minister, I'm not being pessimistic. I'm just being realistic and trying- Well, you're talking about the capacity of the public service having reduced, that

somehow they need 1.2% of the population- No, that's not what I'm saying... to do what they previously did with 1%. That's- I take a more optimistic view. They, like everyone else, are increasing their productivity, increasing their efficiency, increasing their imaginations. That's not what I'm saying, just to be clear. What I am saying is trying to, or rather what I'm asking, I'm trying to probe and understand whether your transformation plan, which we still don't really have the details of, will continue if it is, for example, potentially

proven, as we're seeing in much commentary overseas, that AI is not meeting the transformational potential that it has sold to potentially buy for. Well, as I set out in my primary answer, this isn't just about AI. Mm-hmm. It's actually about reducing duplication, reducing the spaghetti, using digital tools, modernizing the structures which the public service operates within.

And you can guarantee that that will not result in a reduction- But as I said-... of the public service? I am very much of the view that the public service, the core public service, is not less able today than it was a decade ago when it managed to run at 1% of the population. I see no impediment to why it can't do that again. Okay. So for- Thank you. Last up then.

On the AI point, because this has been raised a number of times, including at the point of the announcement, who is going to own that artificial intelligence that's running through the public sector? Respectfully, that wasn't my qu- my question was, who is going to own that AI? And that has been really successful. Great stuff. Thank you. Thank you. Deborah Russell, you had a supplementary? I do have a supp on this. It just goes back to the discussion around food banks and the like. And I guess we funded that food infrastructure during COVID, which was an emergency, and amazing work went on right across communities to make sure that people were fed.

I guess one of the things I find worrying is that we are now industrializing or having an industrialized setup for food rescue and for food banks. So that instead of food banks being very much an emergency temporary measure, as they were 30-odd years ago, when churches ran them and like, they've now become part of our social welfare network, and we actually have industrialized them to an extent.

So instead of being an emergency measure like COVID, it's now industrialized, and that seems to be a very poor reflection on how we're managing the welfare of New Zealanders. Well, you know what? We do have in place a benefit system which ensures that they can access income both for themselves and their dependents in times of need. And it has been our judgment that having a network available so that there is some support for people when they require hardship assistance is justified. But I think that actually focus on the core welfare system is appropriate, and which is why we have not given in to calls from the Greens and at times your own colleagues, who have said, "Put more money just into food banks."

We've said, well, actually, there's a role in terms of that core background infrastructure. But overall, let's focus actually on allowing New Zealanders to be independent, and allowing them to have sufficiency of income. Number one way to ensure sufficiency of income, get people into work, which again takes us back to this discussion. You've got to be growing the economy, creating adequate incentives for people to enter the workforce, so they're not trapped in welfare. And ensuring that the welfare system does its job, which is

always meant to help you on your way, not trap you there forever. With respect, Minister, I've made a habit ever since I became an MP actually and realized some of the difficult things going on with unscrupulous lenders of visiting budget services and money counselors and the like.

And in the visits I've made, not just in the last year or so, they've talked about families, households where there are two adults in work, and those people are now having to visit food banks. It seems the work itself may not-- There's something wrong.

We've got something wrong in the balance of what we're paying people. So if we've got families that are in work having to access food banks, we've got something wrong with the structure of the general expenses in New Zealand. Yeah, look, I fully acknowledge that there are people who find themselves in very difficult circumstances who are working. And that is troubling not just to you, but I think to probably everyone in Parliament.

And that will reflect a range of circumstances. Sometimes it will be that something completely outside of that family's control has happened that's created a significant financial setback, whether that's a large expense that they weren't expecting landing on their lap, and then them going into debt to be able to fund that expense, and then that debt cycle emerging, affecting their ability to access credit, affecting their repayments on a weekly or monthly basis. That's something that you'll hear budget services say, that

sometimes it's one setback that then sets in chain an ongoing picture that can be very difficult for people. It is also the case, of course, that in recent times in New Zealand, that period of 33 months where inflation was completely outside of the target band, where it reached that high of over 7%, meant that overall, the cost base for many working people was accelerating far faster than their wages were. And that has meant that for our government, we've come in on a mission to say, well, the first thing you got to do is get inflation back

under control, and then drive real wage growth so that we can close that gap between what was essentially a significant structural lift in the cost of living under the last government post-COVID, and make sure that people feel that they can get ahead again because real wages are growing faster than inflation. And fundamentally, it's my view that if you want working people to be better off, you need a situation where you're making this country more affordable through wage increases that outpace price increases, and through ensuring that people have the opportunity through their own

efforts to get ahead. So for me, that's about people being able to access promotions, access new jobs, and again, it's why I come back to GDP growth, not because I love being a particular place in a GDP league table, but because with GDP growth comes more jobs, more real incomes, more opportunities. And, I, like you, feel very sympathetic to any family that finds themselves in the position that they're working hard, but they're still finding it really difficult to get ahead.

Of course, at the moment, wage growth is lagging inflation. Look, as I've said, we have a real significant global price shock occurring because of events in the Middle East, and- Oh, no, this was happening before. Sorry. Sorry. Events before. Thank you. We're going to go to a new primary now from Tom Rutherford. Great. The budget had a capital expenditure package of around $7 billion of new capital spending, offset by about $1.3 billion of savings for a net capital impact package of $5.7 billion. How did you decide on the amount of capital?

Well, look, what we saw in this year's budget was a real commitment by this government to continue our investment in the infrastructure pipeline. There has been discussion across the parliament and across our business and wider community about the need for New Zealand to have a longer-term approach to funding infrastructure.

So on becoming government, we set about determining those long-term plans. So in education, we've set a multi-year plan for what investment's required. In health, we've done the same. Across a number of government agencies, we've required a sense of what does the pipeline look like. Then we've set, as I discussed with opposition members earlier, some clearer requirements around the circumstances in which we'll fund infrastructure. So clearer requirements for business cases, for consenting, for deliverability.

When we then looked at the overall capital demands coming into this year's budget, we wanted to square up those long-term pipelines with what was deliverable, what had good business cases, and what was still achievable within our debt ceiling. And that is what ultimately determined the shape of the overall capital budget.

Some people say, "Well, you should just invest whatever you want in capital, that it's fine to borrow for capital." There are two real constraints on that, and we've experienced them in recent times. The first is you can get into a situation if you over-commit to capital, that you end up just inflating the cost of construction.

There's evidence that that occurred under the last government, but also you get delays in construction because actually there's not readiness to deliver that infrastructure. So there are limits there. The other very material limit is around debt. So, while capital is neutral for the overall ex position, it's also the case that it does add to the debt profile.

And so, as you know, we've set that goal of getting the debt curve bending down, and we have to make sure that our capital allocation decisions are consistent with that. Can you just highlight some of the projects included in the capital expenditure program? Yeah. Look, very much making material progress on our health infrastructure pipeline. So you're seeing a new tower block for Whangarei Hospital. And I give tribute there to Dr.

Shane Reti, who has argued for that for many, many years. The next stage of work to redevelop our regional provincial hospitals, Tauranga, Palmerston North, Hawke's Bay, as well as other work to continue infrastructure development for health facilities. Significant investment for education, both to enable new classrooms, the redevelopment of multiple schools across the country that are worn out and old and need redevelopment. Purchasing of land in high-growth areas to allow for new schools to be developed.

Then significant investment in transport infrastructure. In particular, we made a decision to directly accelerate and fund road and resilience projects needed in areas that have become highly affected by weather events, where we just don't think it makes sense to keep rebuilding those roads and billing the taxpayer for that. Let's actually strengthen them now, make them more resilient to future weather events. So $400 million invested in those projects across the country. As well as putting the funding commitment to

allow the very, very much delayed, much talked about extension to the Waikato Expressway to happen, the Cambridge to Piarere extension. That's one of the most unsafe stretches of state highway in the country, as the member from Tauranga will appreciate. Sorry, Bay of Plenty, but you know the Tauranga region, I mean.

That road, on average, has some sort of disruption or closure every 10 days, and that is ridiculous on a piece of road that is so important to the country's productivity. And also has literally meant the loss of life for New Zealanders and their families. So we're very proud that we've committed that additional funding to getting that road going. And then, of course, there's also a chunk of capital there for defense People often highlight the kit that we're buying from offshore, and yes, that's part of the capability, but actually a significant part of this year's

investments are also in the facilities that we ask our servicemen and women to live and train in. And these are men and women who literally put their lives on the line for the country, and I think they deserve to live in housing of a basic standard and to train in facilities that are basic and meet their needs. So, investment there.

Two other projects were new police stations in Greymouth and- Yes, sorry, I didn't mention the courthouses and the police stations- Yeah... and various other things, yeah. But with police stations in Greymouth and Wanganui- Yes... in particular. Why was it necessary to upgrade those facilities in those communities? Yeah. In line with our long-term infrastructure pipeline planning approach, we asked the New Zealand Police to go away and give us a picture of which police stations across the country need to be remediated, need to be replaced, and to have a much more logical

pathway for how that's happening, rather than a really ad hoc approach. They've done a really good business case on that, and very clearly, the next two cabs off the rank in highest need were Greymouth and Wanganui. And therefore, based on that business casing and that pipeline work, that's where we've provided additional investment. And frankly, our police deserve it, as do the people who use those police stations.

Quick sub, Nancy Lou. A very, very quick one. Minister, the big numbers, the big amounts for the capital projects are exciting, and it provides a lot of guarantee and assurance for the construction community. But what does it translate to day-to-day New Zealanders in terms of certainty for jobs- Mm... and commitment into growing that career?

Yeah. Look, I think the first lens is always, what are the infrastructure needs that we have in order to have both the delivery of good public services and the enablement of a good, productive economy. And that's number one. So for me, actually having adequate hospital facilities is pretty core to the delivery of health services, adequate places for our kids to learn and those things.

And then the secondary layer on that is actually when we're building that infrastructure and developing that infrastructure, as you say, that provides jobs, incomes, contract opportunities for New Zealand firms and New Zealand households. And so, I think for your average Kiwi, what they will see in many, many communities across the country is they will see people in hard hats and hi-vis with spades in their hands and jobs building infrastructure for the future.

Infrastructure for the future that will improve public service delivery in the future, will strengthen our economy for the future, but is providing real jobs right now. And I had this highlighted to me by someone the other day who lives in the Hutt, actually, and they were saying, "Yeah, I really can feel that happening," because of course they drive past the Melling development regularly. Mr. Chair, it's 11:04. So- It's a shortie. Sorry? It's a short one. Well, we're due to finish at- I thought we finished at 11:15... 11:15. Oh, 11:15. Okay. So last- It's a long session, isn't it?
